figures.caption: Fig. 2. Schematic illustration of four representative decoder fusion methods: (a) summation; (b) concatenation; (c) selective attention; (d) crossattention. Here, G and L denote the global features from the top-down decoder and the local features from the bottom-up encoder, respectively. F represents the fused output feature. The symbols u⃝, c⃝, ⊕, ⊗, and σ denote upsampling, concatenation, element-wise summation, element-wise multiplication, and sigmoid activation, respectively.

Fig. 5. Architecture of the GG block. In the FDG module, ∆n and ∆p are instantiated as absolute feature differences; in the EDG module, they are defined as signed entropy differences that reflect relative certainty.
Fig. 6. Overall processing pipelines for the medical image segmentation and cloud-removal tasks.

Fig. 9. The diagram illustrates the separator structure of TDANet [7] and the Multi-Scale Attention (MSA) module within TIGER’s separator [8]. While these specific sub-modules share a similar U-Net-like backbone, their top-level global processing differs (a Transformer for TDANet and a feed-forward network for TIGER). Their original fusion modules, which rely on a selective attention mechanism, are replaced with the proposed EDG module. The dimensions annotated in the diagrams represent the output sizes of the convolutional layers.
TABLE 2 Comparison of computational efficiency of different fusion methods in U-Net [1].

TABLE 4 Performance comparison on the ACDC dataset. Metrics include average DSC (%) and HD95 (mm) across three cardiac structures, along with per-class DSC. All models were re-implemented and trained under identical experimental settings. Bold indicates the best result; underlined denotes the second best. The numbers in red indicate absolute differences (in percentage points for DSC and millimeters for HD95) relative to the original TransAttUNet and U-Net baselines.

Fig. 10. Visual analysis of the per-position entropy (X̂p, Eq. (7)) on the Synapse dataset. The entropy maps are extracted from the highest-resolution decoder layer and overlaid as heatmaps on the input images. Red indicates high entropy (low certainty); blue indicates low entropy (high certainty).

TABLE 6 Quantitative comparison of cloud removal performance between our proposed PMAA-EDG and existing models on two cloud removal benchmarks: Sen2 MTC Old and Sen2 MTC New. The best results are bolded, and the second-best results are underlined. The numbers in red denote absolute differences relative to the PMAA [5] baseline (dB for PSNR; unitless for SSIM, FID, LPIPS).

Fig. 11. Qualitative comparison of different methods on the Synapse dataset. From left to right: (a) ground truth, (b) U-Net-EDG (ours), (c) U-Net [1], (d) TransAttUNet-EDG (ours), (e) TransAttUNet [28], (f) AttnUNet [2], and (g) MANet [25]. The red rectangles highlight specific regions of interest to facilitate the comparison of segmentation details. All samples were generated using the checkpoints with the best validation performance.

Fig. 12. Visual comparison of cloud-removal results produced by different methods on two benchmarks: (a) Sen2 MTC Old and (b) Sen2 MTC New. The compared methods include STGAN [37], CTGAN [38], PMAA [5], and PMAA-EDG (ours), alongside the cloudy input images (T1, T2, T3) and the corresponding cloud-free ground-truth images. The red rectangles are used to highlight specific regions of interest, allowing for a clearer visual comparison of different methods.
